title: "Crimson Labyrinth Puzzle v11407"

description: |
  At 22:05 the Crimson Labyrinth Relic vanished from its locked pedestal.
  Four badge-holders (1 2 3 4) were logged nearby.
  Exactly one suspect lies in every statement they utter; the other three are perfectly truthful.
  Exactly one suspect is guilty.
  The universal liar and the thief are different people.

  Suspect 1
    (1) “If every word of 2 is false, then 3 was not at the pedestal.”
    (2) “Statement 3-(2) has the same truth value as my line (3).”
    (3) “Either 4 is the liar xor 2 lingered after 22:00.”
    (4) “I am not the thief.”

  Suspect 2
    (1) “I was at the pedestal at 22:05.”
    (2) “Every statement by 3 is false.”
    (3) “4 is innocent.”
    (4) “If 4 was present, then 1 must be the liar.”

  Suspect 3
    (1) “I was nowhere near that pedestal after 22:00.”
    (2) “Line 2-(3) is wrong.”
    (3) “If 4 tells the truth, then 1 was at the pedestal.”
    (4) “Unless 2 is the thief, 4 certainly is not lying.”

  Suspect 4
    (1) “I am the thief.”
    (2) “I was indeed present at 22:05.”
    (3) “Either 2 is lying xor 1 is the thief.”

question: "Who stole the Crimson Labyrinth Relic? Return a single digit 1–4."

answer: >
  explanation: |
    Assume $L1$.

    $1$–$(3)$ is false, so $L4$ and $P2$ must have the same truth value.

    $4$–$(3)$ is $L2 \oplus G1$ and is true since suspect $4$ is truthful.
    If $L2$ is false, then $G1$ must be true. $1$–$(4)$ is $\lnot G1$, which would be false. But $1$ is the liar, so this aligns.
    However, $L1$ and $G1$ would both be true, violating the constraint $Lx \ne Gx$.
    If $L2$ is true, we have two liars.
    So $L1$ is invalid.

    Assume $L2$.

    $2$–$(1)$ is false, so $P2$ is false.
    $2$–$(2)$ is false, so $L3$ is false.
    Therefore $T3$ is true.
    $2$–$(3)$ is false, so $G4$ is true.
    $2$–$(4)$ is false, so $P4$ is true and $L1$ is false. Therefore $T1$ is true.

    Check suspect $3$:

    $3$–$(2)$ is $\lnot S_{2,3}$.
    $S_{2,3}$ is $\lnot G4$.
    $G4$ is true, so $S_{2,3}$ is false, hence $\lnot S_{2,3}$ is true.

    $3$–$(3)$ is $\lnot L4 \implies P1$.
    $L4$ is not yet fixed. Set $L4$ false. Then $\lnot L4$ is true, so $P1$ must be true.

    $3$–$(4)$ is $\lnot G2 \implies \lnot L4$.
    $G2$ is false, so $\lnot G2$ is true. $L4$ is false, so $\lnot L4$ is true. The implication holds.

    Check suspect $4$:

    $4$–$(1)$ is $G4$, which is true.
    $4$–$(2)$ is $P4$, which is true.
    $4$–$(3)$ is $L2 \oplus G1$.
    $L2$ is true, $G1$ is false. The XOR evaluates to true.

    Check suspect $1$:

    $1$–$(1)$ is $L2 \implies \lnot P3$.
    $L2$ is true, so $\lnot P3$ must be true. Then $P3$ is false.

    $1$–$(2)$ is $S_{3,2} \leftrightarrow S_{1,3}$.
    $3$–$(2)$ is $\lnot S_{2,3}$, and $S_{2,3}$ is $\lnot G4$.
    $G4$ is true, so $S_{2,3}$ is false, hence $\lnot S_{2,3}$ is true.
    $S_{3,2}$ is true.

    $1$–$(3)$ is $L4 \oplus P2$.
    $L4$ is false, $P2$ is false. XOR of two false inputs is false.

    $1$–$(4)$ is $\lnot G1$.
    $G1$ is false, so the statement is true.

    All suspects are consistent under the assignment:

    $L2 = \text{true}$
    $G4 = \text{true}$
    $P2 = \text{false}$
    $P4 = \text{true}$
    $L1 = L3 = L4 = \text{false}$
    $G1 = G2 = G3 = \text{false}$

    No other $Lx$ or $Gx$ is true.
    Liar and thief are distinct.

  final: '4'
